title: Symmetry-Protected Quantum Computing using Metamaterials
authors: Neil F. Johnson, Ferney J. Rodriguez, Luis Quiroga
abstract: We propose a new architecture for practical quantum computing that combines three established principles: symmetry protection of relative-motion qubits via the generalized Kohn theorem, control via twisted-light orbital angular momentum, and metamaterial nanofocusing (e.g. using Weyl-semimetal plasmonics). Crucially, the core mechanism is generic: it applies to any current or future quantum computing system involving parabolic confinement, including cold atoms, ions, and semiconductor dots.
summary: This paper outlines a novel, general architecture for quantum computing that achieves noise protection by leveraging the generalized Kohn theorem in parabolic confinement. It integrates this protection with precise control using twisted-light orbital angular momentum and solves the physical integration challenge using metamaterial nanofocusing on Weyl semimetals. The resulting framework is highly portable, applicable to diverse systems like cold atoms and semiconductor dots.
main_problem: Proposing a general, practical architecture for quantum computing that combines symmetry protection, structured light control, and nanofocusing to overcome current limitations in qubit coherence and addressability.
main_result: The architecture successfully unifies the generalized Kohn theorem (symmetry protection), twisted-light OAM control, and metamaterial nanofocusing into a coherent, portable framework for quantum information processing.
method: The approach combines theoretical analysis of symmetry protection (Kohn theorem) with advanced concepts from quantum optics (OAM-carrying light) and condensed matter physics (Weyl semimetal plasmonics).
model_or_system: The system is modeled using interacting particles confined by a parabolic potential, applicable to semiconductor quantum dots, cold atoms, and trapped ions. The qubit encoding utilizes relative-motion correlation sectors.
key_observables: Qubit splitting frequency ($\hbar\omega_{	ext{qubit}}$), OAM selection rules ($\Delta|m|$), and the residual quadrupolar noise floor.
important_parameters: Parabolic confinement frequency ($\omega_0$), OAM quantum number ($l+\sigma$), and the characteristic length scales related to the wavelength-pitch mismatch.
main_assumptions: The core assumption is the applicability of the generalized Kohn theorem for protection, and the technical assumption is the realizability of OAM-selective nanofocusing using magnetic Weyl semimetals.
figures_summary: Figure 1 schematically illustrates the architecture: N interacting particles in parabolic confinement ($\omega_0$) controlled by a twisted-light pulse ($\omega_T$) via OAM selection, leaving relative modes protected by the Kohn theorem.
paper_structure: The paper develops the architecture by first establishing the need for general symmetry protection (Kohn theorem), then detailing the control mechanism using OAM-light, and finally solving the integration challenge via metamaterial nanofocusing, presenting a portable framework applicable across multiple physical platforms.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant as it proposes a unified, platform-agnostic blueprint for fault-tolerant quantum computation, bridging fundamental symmetry principles with cutting-edge photonic and solid-state engineering.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1. Schematic of the symmetry-protected quantum-computing architecture. N interacting quantum particles in a parabolic confinement (frequency ω0). A twisted-light pulse with orbital angular momentum at central frequency ωT performs WRITE and READ operations through the OAM selection rule ∆|m| = ±(l +σ). Examples of the quantum particles include, but are not limited to, electrons or holes in a semiconductor system. A perpendicular magnetic field B can then be used to manipulate the relative-motion modes.
